## [00:00 - 03:03]

Okay, so there's this one phrase that you can't be in direct marketing for very long without hearing the idea of the USP. The USP is the unique selling proposition. I'm just not a big fan of that. In fact, I don't really know what that means. I guess it means how are you different from everyone else who's in your market? I think it's a good thing. I think if you really take it and run with it, you can probably make some good distinctions that can help you out and you probably want that unique selling proposition. But it's something that just never really resonated. It's something that just never, like I felt like I could take and I could just say USP, USP, and I end up with like, you know, with a nice business. Just never ever really resonated with me. Again, I'm not arguing with it. I'm not saying it's wrong. It's just never really spoke to me. Okay. I have something that I like better that I invented. And it's called the UCP. And that's the unique connection point. And that's, you know, when I started publishing, I published a stock market about the newsletter about the stock market. And there were approximately one bazillion different newsletters about the stock market when I started publishing that newsletter. And there's a lot more now. I mean, it's a very, very competitive market. You'd be hard-pressed to find a single publishing segment that is as crowded as the stock market niche. It's absolutely insane. But I had no problem competing in that market. I had no problem doing very well and building a nice business. Even though I was a complete newbie, a complete and utter newbie when I started out, I'd never sold anything in my life. I'd never published anything in my life. I had no knowledge of sales copy or advertising, of product creation. I just, no, I had no business being in business. I just started publishing. I started sending out a newsletter. And I eventually started charging for that newsletter. And I made, you know, it wasn't millions and millions of dollars,

## [03:03 - 06:03]

but it was, we did at least a million dollars in sales in that business. And I had, you know, I had more money that I could ever have dreamed of before I started that business. It worked out. So how was I able to go into this most competitive of all markets with virtually no experience and virtually no knowledge? How was I able to succeed? How, why did I completely and utterly fail? And I think, I know it was because of the unique connection point. And what the unique connection point is, is it's finding a way to connect with your market and share your personality and who you are. Because people do not like to buy from nameless, faceless corporations. They like to buy from people. They like to, they like to buy from people they know, like and trust. And it's a lot harder to know, like and trust a corporation. It's a lot easier to know, like and trust a person. So in your business, look for your unique, your unique connection point. Try to figure out how you can connect with your market. And a lot of times that's sharing your personality, sharing your experience, sharing your stories. You've noticed as we've gone through this entire course on product creation, I've shared my stories with you right from the very, very beginning. Right from the first video, I started sharing my stories. The second video, my stories. The third video, my stories. My experience in this business. And I guarantee you, if you just stop and think right now, think back over this course and think about like what, what has really stuck in your mind? What have been the most sticky concepts your first time through? I know you're going to go through this a second time and a third time and a fourth time because that's the way you really want to consume one of these, any type of product like this. Any type of training like this, you want to go through it multiple times. But here you are your first time through. Think about what you remember. And I don't know what it is. I don't know what has stuck with you, but I'm willing to bet it was one of the stories. Or it was a point I made through one of my stories. So that's why I've been using stories as we go through here. It's to keep it interesting. It's to keep you engaged, but it's also to make these lessons sticky. You don't want to be boring. You don't want to be dry. You don't want to be corporate. You want to lose the corporate voice. You want to be engaging. You want to be personal. You want to be human. You want to share from your experience and share your stories. And when you start to do that, your products get to have a lot more value because they have a lot more impact on people. But beyond that, you're also going to be able to sell a lot more because you'll make a connection. It's much easier to sell from a personality-based business. In the long run, if you want to take your business and grow your business

## [06:03 - 06:47]

and sell it eventually, selling it from a personality can be a detriment way down the road if you want to sell your business. There are still ways to get around this, but I'm just saying, telling you, in full disclosure, for an end game to sell your business, it can be a little harder if it's a personality-based business. But from a pure selling standpoint and from a teaching standpoint and from an impact standpoint, it's much, much easier and more preferable to sell it when you're putting yourself in it, when you're sharing yourself, sharing your experience, sharing your stories. So something you want to remember, this unique connection point makes business, makes your business a lot easier.
